content.text: Structuring a FastAPI application correctly from the start determines how well it scales — in both team size and codebase complexity — as features accumulate and requirements evolve.

Application architecture in FastAPI refers to the organizational decisions that determine how code is divided into modules, how components communicate, how concerns are separated, and how the application evolves from a single-file prototype to a production-grade service. Unlike frameworks such as Django, which prescribe a specific project layout, FastAPI is deliberately unopinionated about structure — it provides the building blocks (routers, dependencies, middleware, lifespan hooks) without mandating how they are assembled.
This flexibility is a strength at small scale and a risk at large scale. Without deliberate architectural choices, FastAPI applications tend toward a single flat
that grows without bound, mixing routing, business logic, data access, validation, and configuration in ways that become progressively harder to test, deploy, and reason about.

Architecture decisions compound over time. Early structural choices — where business logic lives, how dependencies are composed, whether routers are isolated from database code — become progressively more expensive to change as a codebase grows. Specific problems that arise from poor architectural decisions include:
where business logic is embedded directly in route handlers
across routes that share no common abstraction
where request/response types, ORM models, and domain objects are entangled
when the application cannot be split into services without massive refactoring
when new team members cannot identify where to add a feature without reading the entire codebase
Good architecture is not about choosing the most complex pattern — it is about matching structure to current complexity while leaving clear paths for growth.
The Progression of FastAPI Application Scale
Stage 1: Single File (Prototypes and Tiny APIs)
For simple APIs with three to five endpoints, a single

# main.py
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException, Depends
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Optional
import sqlite3

app = FastAPI()

class Item(BaseModel):
    name: str
    price: float

@app.get("/items/{item_id}")
async def get_item(item_id: int):
    # Everything in one file is fine at this scale
    return {"item_id": item_id}

@app.post("/items/")
async def create_item(item: Item):
    return item
No abstraction layers, no module system, no configuration classes. At this scale, adding them introduces complexity that slows development without adding value.

Once the application has five or more route groups, distinct authentication, and a real database, a flat file becomes unmanageable. The minimal production structure introduces routers and separation of concerns:

my_api/
├── main.py              # App factory, middleware, lifespan
├── config.py            # Settings management
├── database.py          # Engine, session factory, base
├── models.py            # SQLAlchemy models
├── schemas.py           # Pydantic request/response schemas
├── dependencies.py      # Shared Depends() functions
├── routers/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── users.py
│   ├── items.py
│   └── auth.py
└── tests/
    ├── conftest.py
    └── test_users.py
Stage 3: Domain-Based Structure (Medium to Large APIs)

When the team grows beyond two or three developers, or when the application has more than around fifteen distinct resource types, domain-based organization becomes essential. Code is organized around business domains rather than technical layers:

my_api/
├── main.py
├── config.py
├── database.py
├── core/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── security.py          # Auth utilities, token logic
│   ├── exceptions.py        # Custom exception classes
│   └── middleware.py        # Custom middleware
├── domains/
│   ├── users/
│   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   ├── router.py        # FastAPI router
│   │   ├── schemas.py       # Pydantic models for this domain
│   │   ├── models.py        # SQLAlchemy models
│   │   ├── service.py       # Business logic
│   │   ├── repository.py    # Data access
│   │   └── dependencies.py  # Domain-specific dependencies
│   ├── orders/
│   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   ├── router.py
│   │   ├── schemas.py
│   │   ├── models.py
│   │   ├── service.py
│   │   └── repository.py
│   └── products/
│       └── ...
└── tests/
    ├── conftest.py
    └── domains/
        ├── test_users.py
        └── test_orders.py
This layout means a developer working on the
in the vast majority of cases. Cross-domain dependencies are explicit and visible.

is FastAPI's mechanism for splitting routes across files without coupling them at import time. A router is a mini-application that accumulates route declarations and is later mounted onto the main app.

# main.py
from fastapi import FastAPI
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager

from domains.users.router import router as users_router
from domains.orders.router import router as orders_router
from domains.products.router import router as products_router
from domains.auth.router import router as auth_router
from core.middleware import setup_middleware

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    # Startup: initialize connection pools, caches, background workers
    from database import engine
    app.state.engine = engine
    yield
    # Shutdown: close connections, flush queues
    await engine.dispose()

app = FastAPI(
    title="My API",
    version="1.0.0",
    lifespan=lifespan
)

setup_middleware(app)

# Auth router has no prefix-level auth dependency (it IS the auth entry point)
app.include_router(auth_router, prefix="/auth", tags=["Authentication"])

# All other routers
app.include_router(users_router, prefix="/api/v1")
app.include_router(orders_router, prefix="/api/v1")
app.include_router(products_router, prefix="/api/v1")
does not import route handlers — it copies route definitions into the main application. Multiple apps can include the same router, enabling modular testing.
Configuration should be centralized, type-validated, and loaded from environment variables — never hardcoded or scattered across modules.

# config.py
from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings, SettingsConfigDict
from functools import lru_cache
from typing import Optional

class Settings(BaseSettings):
    model_config = SettingsConfigDict(
        env_file=".env",
        env_file_encoding="utf-8",
        case_sensitive=False,
        extra="ignore"
    )

    # Application
    app_name: str = "My API"
    app_version: str = "1.0.0"
    debug: bool = False
    environment: str = "production"

    # Database
    database_url: str
    db_pool_size: int = 10
    db_max_overflow: int = 20
    db_pool_timeout: int = 30
    db_pool_recycle: int = 1800

    # Auth
    secret_key: str
    access_token_expire_minutes: int = 30
    refresh_token_expire_days: int = 30
    algorithm: str = "HS256"

    # External services
    redis_url: str = "redis://localhost:6379"
    s3_bucket: Optional[str] = None
    aws_region: str = "us-east-1"

    # Rate limiting
    rate_limit_requests: int = 100
    rate_limit_window: int = 60

    @property
    def is_development(self) -> bool:
        return self.environment == "development"

    @property
    def async_database_url(self) -> str:
        """Convert sync URL to async URL if needed."""
        return self.database_url.replace(
            "postgresql://", "postgresql+asyncpg://"
        )

@lru_cache()
def get_settings() -> Settings:
    return Settings()

# Usage anywhere in the app
settings = get_settings()
is only instantiated once — reading environment variables and
files is done at startup, not on every request.
are present, raising clear errors at startup rather than mid-request.

Business logic does not belong in route handlers. Route handlers are responsible for HTTP concerns — extracting request data, calling appropriate service methods, and forming HTTP responses. Service classes encapsulate business rules, cross-cutting operations, and orchestration between repositories.

The service has no knowledge of HTTP, FastAPI, or SQLAlchemy sessions. It operates on domain objects and raises domain-appropriate exceptions. This makes it independently testable without an HTTP layer.

A common architectural error is using the same Pydantic model for input, output, and database representation. Each of these has different fields, different validation rules, and different serialization needs.

# domains/users/schemas.py
from pydantic import BaseModel, EmailStr, Field, ConfigDict
from typing import Optional
from datetime import datetime

# ── Input schemas (what the client sends) ────────────────────────────────────

class UserCreate(BaseModel):
    username: str = Field(..., min_length=3, max_length=50, pattern=r"^[a-zA-Z0-9_]+$")
    email: EmailStr
    password: str = Field(..., min_length=8, max_length=100)

class UserUpdate(BaseModel):
    username: Optional[str] = Field(None, min_length=3, max_length=50)
    email: Optional[EmailStr] = None
    password: Optional[str] = Field(None, min_length=8)
    # Note: no is_active — users cannot deactivate themselves via this schema

class UserLogin(BaseModel):
    email: EmailStr
    password: str

# ── Output schemas (what the API returns) ────────────────────────────────────

class UserResponse(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(from_attributes=True)

    id: int
    username: str
    email: str
    is_active: bool
    created_at: datetime
    # Note: no hashed_password — never returned

class UserSummary(BaseModel):
    """Compact representation for list endpoints and embedded references."""
    model_config = ConfigDict(from_attributes=True)

    id: int
    username: str

class UserAdminView(UserResponse):
    """Extended view for admin endpoints — includes sensitive metadata."""
    last_login: Optional[datetime] = None
    login_count: int = 0
    roles: list[str] = []

# ── Internal schemas ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

class UserInDB(BaseModel):
    """Represents a user as stored — used internally, never returned directly."""
    id: int
    username: str
    email: str
    hashed_password: str    # Present internally but stripped from all API responses
    is_active: bool
    created_at: datetime
what comes in, what goes out, and what lives in the database are three different things
. Even if they share 80% of fields, the 20% difference is where security vulnerabilities and confusing APIs originate.
system is the mechanism for connecting components. Well-designed dependency chains are the backbone of testable, composable FastAPI architecture.
This chain means that in tests,
can be overridden to inject a service backed by a test database or a mock repository, without touching the endpoint code at all.

# tests/conftest.py
import pytest
from httpx import AsyncClient, ASGITransport
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import create_async_engine, async_sessionmaker, AsyncSession

from main import app
from database import get_db
from domains.users.dependencies import get_user_service
from domains.users.service import UserService
from domains.users.repository import UserRepository

TEST_DATABASE_URL = "postgresql+asyncpg://user:password@localhost:5432/test_db"

@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
def event_loop_policy():
    import asyncio
    return asyncio.DefaultEventLoopPolicy()

@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
async def test_engine():
    engine = create_async_engine(TEST_DATABASE_URL, echo=False)
    yield engine
    await engine.dispose()

@pytest.fixture
async def db_session(test_engine):
    """Each test gets a rolled-back transaction."""
    async with test_engine.begin() as conn:
        # Run all tests inside a transaction that is rolled back afterward
        async with AsyncSession(bind=conn, expire_on_commit=False) as session:
            yield session
            await session.rollback()

@pytest.fixture
async def client(db_session):
    """HTTP client with database dependency overridden."""
    async def override_get_db():
        yield db_session

    app.dependency_overrides[get_db] = override_get_db

    async with AsyncClient(
        transport=ASGITransport(app=app),
        base_url="http://test"
    ) as ac:
        yield ac

    app.dependency_overrides.clear()
Consistent, structured error responses require a centralized error handling strategy rather than ad hoc
With this system, service methods raise domain exceptions (
) without knowing anything about HTTP. The handlers translate them to HTTP responses in one centralized location.
The app factory pattern creates the FastAPI instance inside a function, enabling different configurations for testing, development, and production:

# main.py
from fastapi import FastAPI
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager

from config import get_settings, Settings
from core.middleware import setup_middleware
from core.exceptions import setup_exception_handlers
from domains.users.router import router as users_router
from domains.orders.router import router as orders_router
from domains.products.router import router as products_router
from domains.auth.router import router as auth_router

def create_application(settings: Settings | None = None) -> FastAPI:
    if settings is None:
        settings = get_settings()

    @asynccontextmanager
    async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
        # ── Startup ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
        from database import engine, AsyncSessionLocal
        from redis.asyncio import from_url as redis_from_url

        app.state.settings = settings
        app.state.engine = engine
        app.state.redis = await redis_from_url(settings.redis_url)

        yield

        # ── Shutdown ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
        await app.state.engine.dispose()
        await app.state.redis.close()

    application = FastAPI(
        title=settings.app_name,
        version=settings.app_version,
        debug=settings.debug,
        docs_url="/docs" if settings.is_development else None,    # Disable docs in prod
        redoc_url="/redoc" if settings.is_development else None,
        lifespan=lifespan,
    )

    setup_middleware(application, settings)
    setup_exception_handlers(application)

    application.include_router(auth_router, prefix="/auth", tags=["Authentication"])
    application.include_router(users_router, prefix="/api/v1")
    application.include_router(orders_router, prefix="/api/v1")
    application.include_router(products_router, prefix="/api/v1")

    return application

app = create_application()
Versioning strategies determine how breaking changes are introduced without disrupting existing clients.

from fastapi import APIRouter

v1_router = APIRouter(prefix="/api/v1")
v2_router = APIRouter(prefix="/api/v2")

# V1 routes
from domains.users.router import router as users_v1
v1_router.include_router(users_v1)

# V2 routes — may reuse v1 routers for unchanged resources
from domains.users.v2.router import router as users_v2
v2_router.include_router(users_v2)

app.include_router(v1_router)
app.include_router(v2_router)

# domains/users/router.py — v1 behavior
router_v1 = APIRouter(prefix="/users", tags=["Users v1"])

@router_v1.get("/{user_id}")
async def get_user_v1(user_id: int, ...):
    # Returns flat user object
    ...

# domains/users/v2/router.py — v2 behavior
router_v2 = APIRouter(prefix="/users", tags=["Users v2"])

@router_v2.get("/{user_id}")
async def get_user_v2(user_id: int, ...):
    # Returns user object with nested address and preferences
    ...

URL path versioning is the most operationally straightforward — it is visible in logs, cacheable by CDNs, and clear in client code. Header versioning is cleaner aesthetically but harder to observe and route at the infrastructure level.
A multi-domain SaaS application with auth, billing, and content domains:

# Project layout
"""
saas_api/
├── main.py
├── config.py
├── database.py
├── core/
│   ├── security.py
│   ├── exceptions.py
│   ├── middleware.py
│   └── pagination.py
├── domains/
│   ├── auth/
│   │   ├── router.py
│   │   ├── schemas.py
│   │   └── service.py
│   ├── billing/
│   │   ├── router.py
│   │   ├── schemas.py
│   │   ├── models.py
│   │   ├── service.py
│   │   ├── repository.py
│   │   └── webhooks.py       # Stripe webhook handlers
│   └── content/
│       ├── router.py
│       ├── schemas.py
│       ├── models.py
│       ├── service.py
│       └── repository.py
└── tests/
    ├── conftest.py
    ├── test_auth.py
    └── domains/
        ├── test_billing.py
        └── test_content.py
"""

# core/pagination.py — shared pagination utility
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Generic, TypeVar, List

T = TypeVar("T")

class PaginatedResponse(BaseModel, Generic[T]):
    items: List[T]
    total: int
    skip: int
    limit: int
    has_next: bool
    has_prev: bool

    @classmethod
    def create(cls, items: list, total: int, skip: int, limit: int):
        return cls(
            items=items,
            total=total,
            skip=skip,
            limit=limit,
            has_next=(skip + limit) < total,
            has_prev=skip > 0
        )

# domains/billing/router.py
from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends, Request, HTTPException, status
from domains.billing.schemas import (
    SubscriptionCreate, SubscriptionResponse,
    InvoiceResponse, WebhookPayload
)
from domains.billing.service import BillingService
from domains.billing.dependencies import get_billing_service
from core.security import get_current_user
from core.pagination import PaginatedResponse

router = APIRouter(prefix="/billing", tags=["Billing"])

@router.post("/subscriptions/", response_model=SubscriptionResponse, status_code=201)
async def create_subscription(
    data: SubscriptionCreate,
    current_user = Depends(get_current_user),
    service: BillingService = Depends(get_billing_service)
):
    return await service.create_subscription(current_user.id, data)

@router.get("/invoices/", response_model=PaginatedResponse[InvoiceResponse])
async def list_invoices(
    skip: int = 0,
    limit: int = 20,
    current_user = Depends(get_current_user),
    service: BillingService = Depends(get_billing_service)
):
    invoices, total = await service.list_invoices(current_user.id, skip, limit)
    return PaginatedResponse.create(invoices, total, skip, limit)

@router.post("/webhooks/stripe/", include_in_schema=False)  # Hidden from docs
async def stripe_webhook(
    request: Request,
    service: BillingService = Depends(get_billing_service)
):
    """Stripe calls this endpoint — no auth dependency, verified via signature."""
    payload = await request.body()
    signature = request.headers.get("stripe-signature")

    if not service.verify_stripe_signature(payload, signature):
        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="Invalid webhook signature")

    event = await request.json()
    await service.process_stripe_event(event)
    return {"status": "processed"}

# main.py
from fastapi import FastAPI
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from config import get_settings
from core.middleware import setup_middleware
from core.exceptions import setup_exception_handlers
from domains.auth.router import router as auth_router
from domains.billing.router import router as billing_router
from domains.content.router import router as content_router

def create_application():
    settings = get_settings()

    @asynccontextmanager
    async def lifespan(app):
        from database import engine
        import redis.asyncio as aioredis
        app.state.redis = await aioredis.from_url(settings.redis_url)
        yield
        await engine.dispose()
        await app.state.redis.close()

    app = FastAPI(
        title="SaaS Platform API",
        version="2.0.0",
        lifespan=lifespan,
        docs_url="/docs" if settings.is_development else None,
    )

    setup_middleware(app, settings)
    setup_exception_handlers(app)

    app.include_router(auth_router, prefix="/auth")
    app.include_router(billing_router, prefix="/api/v2")
    app.include_router(content_router, prefix="/api/v2")

    @app.get("/health", include_in_schema=False)
    async def health_check():
        return {"status": "healthy", "version": settings.app_version}

    return app

app = create_application()

# WRONG — route handler doing business logic directly
@router.post("/orders/")
async def create_order(order: OrderCreate, db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db)):
    # Inventory check, price calculation, stock decrement all in the handler
    product = await db.get(Product, order.product_id)
    if product.stock < order.quantity:
        raise HTTPException(400, "Insufficient stock")
    product.stock -= order.quantity
    new_order = Order(...)
    db.add(new_order)
    await db.commit()
    return new_order
Move all of this to a service class. The route handler should call one method and return its result.

# WRONG — creates circular imports
# domains/users/router.py
from main import app

@app.get("/users/")  # Bypasses router system
async def list_users():
    ...
file with 30 SQLAlchemy classes creates merge conflicts on every PR and makes it impossible to understand which models belong to which domain. Co-locate models with their domain.

# WRONG — scattered, untestable, insecure
@router.post("/login/")
async def login(credentials: LoginRequest):
    token = jwt.encode(payload, "my-secret-key", algorithm="HS256")  # Hardcoded
    ...
, every endpoint returns whatever the function returns — including ORM internals, relationship proxies, and fields that should never be exposed (like
). Every endpoint should have an explicit

# WRONG — shared mutable state across requests and workers
connected_users = {}  # Not safe across multiple workers

@app.websocket("/ws/{user_id}")
async def ws(user_id: str, websocket: WebSocket):
    connected_users[user_id] = websocket  # Mutates global state
, and Redis for state that must be shared across workers.
Organize by domain, not by technical layer
containing router, service, repository, and schemas beats
— they should do three things: extract request data, call a service method, return a response. Anything else belongs in the service layer.
should be a clear, testable chain with each layer injectable.
— creating the FastAPI instance in a function (not at module level) enables configuration-driven test and production variants.
prefix costs nothing and makes future versioning straightforward.
— define domain exceptions and register exception handlers in one place rather than scattering
are always different, even when they share most fields.
for internal endpoints (health checks, internal webhooks, metrics) to keep the public API docs clean.
with required fields ensures missing environment variables fail fast at startup, not mid-request.
— connection pools, Redis clients, and other long-lived objects belong in
FastAPI is deliberately unopinionated about structure — the developer must impose architecture deliberately, or the codebase degrades into an unstructured monolith.
The correct organizational unit is the
, not the technical layer — co-locating router, service, repository, schemas, and models per domain scales better than segregating all routers together and all services together.
is the architectural boundary between HTTP concerns and business logic — route handlers extract and validate HTTP input, service methods implement domain rules, repositories handle persistence.
is the tool for modularizing routes — it enables independent development, testing, and potential extraction to separate services.
instance) enables clean test isolation and environment-specific configuration without global state.
provides type-validated, environment-variable-driven configuration with startup-time validation — hardcoded values and scattered
between input, output, and database representation is non-negotiable — conflating them is the source of both security vulnerabilities (exposing
) are the testability backbone of FastAPI —
enables full test isolation without mocking at the module level.
